title: The main tradeoff of stateless JWTs
prompt_md: A stateless **JWT** carries the user's claims (like `user:42, role:admin`) signed by the server, and the server stores no session data. Compared with classic server-side sessions, what is the main tradeoff?
options: JWTs can't carry any user information, so you must hit the database on every request anyway
Because nothing is stored server-side, you can't easily revoke a JWT before it expires (e.g. on logout or ban)
JWTs are encrypted, so the client can never read what's inside them
JWTs require a database lookup on every request, making them slower than sessions
explanation_md: With a **server session** the server holds the state, so revoking access is trivial — delete the session row and the user is logged out instantly. A **stateless JWT** is trusted purely because its signature verifies, and the server stores nothing to delete. So until the token **expires**, the server keeps honoring it — you can't cleanly revoke it on logout or ban without adding extra machinery (like a denylist), which reintroduces the very server-side state JWTs were meant to avoid. That's why JWTs use short expiry times. Note a JWT payload is only *signed*, not encrypted — anyone can read it, so never put secrets in it.
